data.title: Week Ahead: Impasses Between U.S. And China And Between Republicans And Democrats
data.description:
There are several moving pieces in the broad macro story that are not going to be resolved in the coming days. Although President Trump is playing down a "trade war" with China, it is difficult to see the basis for a deal when he meets with China's Xi at the end of the month in South Korea. The US is not about to relax its chokehold on advanced semiconductor technology or allow subsidiaries of sanctioned Chinese companies to provide a loophole. Nor does it appear likely that Beijing would jettison its export licensing requirements for rare earths, processing technology, and EV battery technology. It also does not seem that either side is prepared to backtrack on its port fees levied on the other. The US federal government remains largely shut, and the path to reopening remains elusive. A federal court has blocked the "reduction in force", permanent dismissals.

The fraud-related write-downs at two regional US banks, on the heels of the collapse of Tricolor and First Brands that adversely impacted several large investors, were a shot across the bow. It is a useful reminder of the unresolved risks emanating from commercial real estate. Office loan delinquencies have reached 10.4%, approaching 2008 levels. Around $1 trillion of CRE loans must be refinanced before year-end, according to industry reports. With its gain of about 1.8% before the weekend, the KBW Regional Bank Index fell by around 1.6% last week. It has fallen for four consecutive weeks, which is the longest losing streak this year. The week ahead highlights include US CPI (yes), Japan's Diet selecting a new prime minister, China reporting Q3 GDP, and holding the 4th plenary session to frame the next five-year plan. The preliminary October PMI will also be released.

US

Drivers: With what seems to be the end of the fragile and tentative trade truce between the US and China, the average effective US tariff is likely to jump by 8-13 percentage points depending on if the reciprocal product exemptions remain. The market recognizes this as a headwind for the US economy. The futures market has a Fed cut fully discounted for later this month and in December and has begun to rethink the possibility of a 50 bp cut at one of the last two meetings of the year. The large write-offs at two regional banks brought back to the fore late-cycle credit fears. The commercial real estate (CRE) market remains at the epicenter. CRE loans account for an estimated 44% of regional bank portfolios compared with around 13% for large banks. Office loan delinquencies are near 10.5%, approaching 2008 levels. Over $1 trillion of CRE loans need to be refinanced by year-end according to some estimates.

Data: With the federal government still closed, regional Fed surveys, the preliminary October PMI, and existing home sales are on tap. However, at the end of the week, the BLS has said it will report the September CPI. The exception is being made because of the importance of the report in setting the cost-of-living adjustment for government programs, including social security. Still, with many Fed officials, though clearly not all, wanting to look through what is thought to be tariff-induced price pressures, the main focus remains on the labor market. That said, headline CPI is rising at a 2.4% annualized pace through August, though the increase in Q3 looks to be more than in Q1 and Q2.

Prices: The Dollar Index settled on October 10 slightly below 99.00. It was sold for almost 98.00 at the end of last week. The rally from the multi-year low set when the Fed cut interest rates on September 17 (~96.20) to around 99.50 ended. With last week's pullback, the Dollar Index approached the (50%) retracement found near 97.90. It recovered to 98.55 before the weekend. Nearby resistance is in the 98.60-75 area. The daily momentum indicators are turning down in overbought territory.

EMU

Drivers: The mainstream narrative casts Europe as being squeezed between Russia's hybrid warfare tactics, the nationalistic US, and China's rare earth dominance. The euro itself is caught between poor German (and French economic data) and the escalation of US-Chinese tensions. The euro's inverse correlation over the past 30 and 60 sessions with the US two-year yield is greater than the inverse correlation with changes in the two-year spread with Germany.

Data: The markets seem more sensitive to the flash PMI than to the current account figures that are also due. Recall that in September, the composite PMI stood at 51.2, its highest level since May 2024. It rose in the four months through September. It finished last year at 49.6. Ahead of the weekend, S&P downgraded France from AA- to A+, matching an earlier move by Fitch. Moody's, the last of the big three rating agencies that ascribes a AA- equivalent to France (Aa3) will announce the results of its review on October 24. Separately, DBRS lifted Italy's rating to A (low) from BBB (high). It is the highest rating from any major company. Moody's rates Italy Baa3 (=BBB-) and has a positive outlook and will update it next month.

Prices: The euro has been trending lower after recording a multi-year high near $1.1920 on September 17, when the Federal Reserve cut rates. It forged a bottom around $1.1540 and reached almost $1.1730 before the weekend, the (50%) retracement of the losses since September 17. The euro stalled and found support around $1.1650. After the low was recorded, the euro was unable to reclaim the $1.1680. It settled near session lows. Nearby support is seen in the $1.1630-40 area. The momentum indicators are turning over in oversold territory.

China

Drivers: Beijing remains committed to the yuan's shadowing of the dollar. In a weak dollar environment, the yuan typically loses ground against other currencies, and in a stronger US dollar environment, the yuan is among the better performers. Given the escalation of tensions between Washington and Beijing, there may be more speculation that the exchange rate could be weaponized. The US dollar did jump around the so-called "Liberation Day" in early April. It reached the year's high on April 8 near CNH7.43 (and ~CNY7.35). By the time the first tariff truce was announced (May 12), the dollar was around CNH7.20-CNH7.25. When the extension was announced on August 11, the dollar was about CNH7.18-CNH7.20. Yet, the PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at a new low for the year last week. It was set at CNY7.0949 before the weekend, and CNY7.1048 at the end of the previous week.

Data: Owing to the close management of the exchange rate, the yuan does not appear particularly sensitive to Chinese data. In the week ahead, the most important report is the Q3 GDP and the real sector performance in September. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey for Q3 GDP is 0.8% quarter-over-quarter after 1.1% growth in Q2. That would be the first sub-1% quarter since Q4 23. Year-over-year growth is seen near 4.7% (vs. 5.2%). If accurate, it would be the slowest pace since the end of 2022. It would likely spur speculation of more stimulus measures. The fourth plenum session of the Communist Party begins Monday. This is usually the forum to discuss the next five-year plan and announce personnel changes.

Prices: The greenback briefly traded at a new low for the month, near CNH7.1170 before the weekend. It recovered and traded at CNH7.1325 to edge above the previous day's high. It settled within Thursday's range. It still looks broadly range-bound.

Japan

Drivers: The easy monetary and easy fiscal policy advocated by the new head of the LDP overwhelmed other drivers of the exchange rate. Still, the combination of MOF warnings about a one-sided rapid movement in the exchange rate coupled with the sharp drop in US yields amid the escalation of tensions with China effectively arrested the yen's sharp slide. The dollar has been correcting lower. On October 21, the Diet will pick a new prime minister. The opposition parties have failed to unite behind a single candidate, which means that the most likely scenario is for LDP's new leader Takaichi to be elected and become the first woman prime minister of Japan.

Data: There are three data points that attract the market's interest in the week ahead, and while they pose headline risk, they are unlikely to overwhelm other considerations. The first is the September trade balance. The fact of the matter is that despite the yen being undervalued on nearly any metric, it experiences a trade deficit. This would seem to have implications for other currencies, such as the yuan, which are understood to be less undervalued than the yen. In the first eight months of the year, Japan reported a nearly JPY2.6 trillion trade deficit compared with a JPY4.7 trillion deficit in the same period last year, and JPY8.1 trillion in Jan-Aug 2023. The second data point is the September CPI. Yet, we know from the Tokyo CPI that there will be little change in the headline and core measures, but that measure that excludes both fresh food and energy may converge with the headline and core rates. It stood at 3.3% in August, while the headline and core were at 2.7%. Third is the flash PMI. Typically, the local market does not put much weight on it. Still, for the record, the composite in September fell to 51.3 (from 52.0), the lowest since May. It finished last year at 50.5.

Prices: The dollar recorded a bearish key reversal on October 10 by making a new high for the move (~JPY153.25) and then selling off to close (~JPY151.20) below the previous day's low. Follow-through selling took it slightly below JPY149.40 before the weekend. Yet, as US interest rates recovered, so did the greenback, which recorded new session highs in early North American turnover, slightly above JPY150.60. The recovery and strong close look like bullish dollar price action. The greenback held above the key technical area marked by the gap that was created by the dollar's higher opening on October 6. The gap extends from the high on October 3 (~JPY147.80) to the October 6 low (~JPY149.05). Nearby resistance is seen in the JPY151.00-40 area.

UK

Drivers: Over the past 30 sessions, the correlation between changes in the euro and sterling is near 0.85, near the highest since the year's peak in June, slightly above 0.92. The year's low was recorded in late July near 0.65. Sterling's inverse correlation with the Dollar Index is slightly higher at around -0.88. Meanwhile, over the past 30 sessions, sterling's inverse correlation with changes in the US two-year (~-0.47) and its inverse correlation with changes in the UK's two-year yield have converged around (~-0.49).

Data: The UK reports CPI, retail sales, and sees the preliminary October PMI. The bar to another cut this year (November 6 and December 8 BOE meetings) is high. A key reason is that inflation has accelerated to a 3.6% annualized rate in August compared with a 2.4% annualized rate in August 2024. However, the culprit is the rise in administered prices, which included energy, water, train fares, and an increase in local authority taxes. Consumers continue to shop. In volume terms, retail sales have risen by an average of 0.3% a month through August. They averaged a 0.5% increase in the first eight months of 2024, after contracting in the Jan-August period of 2022 and 2023. Turning to the PMI, the composite flashed a warning in September, falling sharply from 53.5, the best since August 2024 to 50.1, the weakest since April. The services PMI tumbled to 50.8 from 54.2, while the manufacturing PMI fell to 46.2 from 47.0. It has not been above the 50 boom/bust level since the end of Q3 24.

Prices: Sterling recovered smartly from about $1.3250 on October 14, its lowest level since August 1. It reached $1.3470 before the weekend, a new high for the week. It stalled in front of the (50%) retracement of the decline since the September 17 high (~$1.3725), which is found slightly above $1.3485. Selling pressure pushed sterling to new session lows, and it took out the previous day's low near $1.3390. Nevertheless, it recovered to settle little changed near the middle of the day's range. What appears to be consolidation looks constructive.

Canada

Drivers: The Canadian dollar remains sensitive to the US dollar's overall direction, which may make sense given the relative size of the economies and integration. The low volatility of the exchange rate contributes to the Canadian dollar's better performance with the G10 in a strong US dollar environment. The Canadian dollar also sometimes is a risk-off currency, i.e., especially when US equities trend lower.

Data: The Bank of Canada's Q3 outlook survey on October 20 will likely confirm what the data have already suggested; namely, that economic activity is weak. Canada also reports August retail sales. Preliminary data from StatsCan suggest they recovered from the 0.8% decline in July. Yet the economy looks as if it stagnated in Q3 after contracting in Q2. Canada's CPI has risen at an annualized rate of 3.6% through August, the same pace as seen in the first eight months of 2024. The underlying core measures have accelerated this year. The central bank sees the underlying rate "in the vicinity of 2.5%." Still, market has pared odds of a Bank of Canada rate cut this month and before the end of the year following the stronger than expected September jobs report.

Prices: The US dollar reached a six-month high last week near CAD1.4080. It spent the second half of last week consolidating mostly above CAD1.4020. The level yielded ahead of the weekend. The greenback posted an outside down day by trading on both sides of Thursday's range and settling below its low (~CAD1.4025). On a break of CAD1.40, a push below the CAD1.3960 area would boost the chances a high is in place. The momentum indicators are over-extended and look poised to turn down.

Australia

Drivers: We have been noting the sensitivity of the Australian dollar exchange rate to changes in the US dollar broadly (proxy, the Dollar Index). However, this has changed dramatically. The rolling 30-day inverse correlation has slackened from -0.80 to around -0.27, the least since April 2022. There is an element of an increased role of risk-off as the correlation between changes in the exchange rate and changes in the S&P 500 (~0.74, the highest since January 2024). More difficult to quantify, but the escalation of tension between Australia's military alliances and its largest trading partner appears to also have weighed on the Australian dollar.

Data: In a quiet week for Australian data, the preliminary October PMI is the highlight. The composite PMI fell to 52.4 in September (from 55.5 in August). Yet, the Q3 average of 53.9 is still the best quarter in more than three years. Australia is one of the few high-income countries with a manufacturing PMI above the 50 boom/bust. The futures market has almost a 60% chance of a cut at next month's central bank meeting. At the end of September, the futures market had less than a 1-in-3 chance of a cut.

Prices: The Australian dollar recorded a key downside reversal after the FOMC's rate cut on September 17. It had set a new high for the year, near $0.6710. It has trended lower over the past month and tested the $0.6440 area last week. Ahead of the weekend, the Aussie settled firmly near the session high of $0.6500. The momentum indicators are oversold but have not turned higher. Meanwhile, the $0.6520 area must be overcome to stabilize the technical tone.

Mexico

Drivers: In late September, the rolling 30-day correlation between changes in the USD-MXN exchange rate and the Dollar Index was near 0.83, the highest in more than a decade. It is now a little below 0.30, a five-month low. A risk-off element has strengthened. The inverse correlation between changes in the exchange rate and the S&P 500 is near -0.75. It has not been more sensitive since mid-2022. The exchange rate's sensitivity to changes in the US two-year yield has been modestly positive for the past three months, but now is the most inverse since late May.

Data: Mexico reports the August IGAE measure of economic activity, which is similar to a monthly GDP report. It has contracted in two of the three months through July. Mexico also reports August retail sales. They were flat in June-July. While the economy is generating faint economic impulses, price pressure remains elevated. Mexico reports CPI for the first half of October. Headline CPI in September stood at 3.76%, the highest since June. The core rate stood at 4.28%, the highest since April 2024. The official target is 3% in a 2%-4% range. Banxico has become more focused on the economic weakness, and the swaps market anticipates another quarter-point rate cut before the end of the year.

Prices: With a brief exception on October 16, the US dollar spent last week within the range established on October 10: ~MXN18.3640-MXN18.6370. It tested the low before the weekend. Last week's high was almost MXN18.63. Still, it settled below MXN18.50 without fail. In fact, outside of October 10, the greenback has not settled above MXN18.50 for over a month. Until a clearer macro picture emerges, broad sideways trading seems most likely. The greenback remains in its trough. It bottomed on September 17 near MXN18.20, a new low for the year. The dollar has not traded above MXN19.00 since late June.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Why American Express Is Still Worth Buying For The Long Run
data.description:
Looking at American Express (NYSE:AXP) today, I still feel good about being in the Buy camp. Since the last time I weighed in, the stock has climbed around 9 percent, moving from $317 to $346. That’s a solid jump, especially considering it’s also hit new record highs. Over the past year, shareholders have seen a nice 21 percent gain. After the most recent earnings update, the stock has really shown some strength.

So, why do I think American Express is still a buy, even though the price isn’t exactly cheap right now? To me, the market keeps underestimating just how much pricing power this company really has and how loyal its premium customer base can be. American Express has a reputation that keeps people coming back. Plus, it has proven it can still grow with younger customers and overseas. Some investors might think the easy money is gone but I see a company that is still finding ways to get better. The recent guidance raise and steady credit numbers tell me this isn’t a fluke.

There’s this idea out there that Amex can only go so far, or that competition from new tech players will eat into its business. The numbers say otherwise. Write-offs are steady, people are spending more on their cards and the brand is connecting with new generations and people overseas. I think the story here is about quality and steady results, not chasing a fast buck. Sure, there is a risk that people are paying too much for the stock if the company stumbles. Still, I think Amex has more ways to surprise to the upside and reward investors who stick around for a while.

Bottom line, this isn’t a name I’d expect to get super cheap. But I see American Express as a solid pick for long-term growth, especially if you’re able to buy on small dips.

How American Express Builds Loyalty and Keeps Growing

American Express covers a lot of ground. It issues cards, signs up merchants, runs its own payments network and even offers travel and rewards programs. Its real strength is simple: the brand stands for quality and exclusivity. That draws in customers who like good service and are willing to pay for it. Right now, Amex has over 151 million cards in circulation and its cards are accepted in more places than ever.

Lately, Amex has put a lot of effort into keeping its best customers happy. For example, it recently refreshed its Platinum Cards and raised the annual fees, adding new perks to justify the higher price. Amex is also making moves with tech, such as teaming up with Toast and building out its Amex Ads platform to help brands reach its cardholders. The goal is pretty clear: stay ahead of the curve, hold onto the premium crowd and keep growing in younger and international markets.

All these efforts are showing up in the numbers. Last quarter, Amex added over three million new cards. Average fees per card are climbing and spending from international customers is growing even faster. The company’s top marks in customer satisfaction studies only add to the story.

To me, the takeaway is that Amex is a lot more than just another credit card company. It’s built a business model that is tough to copy and keeps finding ways to get more from its customer relationships.

Why Amex’s Niche Protects It from Disruption

There’s no shortage of change in payments and consumer finance right now. New players are popping up, digital wallets and pay-later options are growing and competition is fierce. But if you look at the wealthiest customers, those who care about rewards and top-tier service, Amex still owns that space.

Other big companies like Capital One and Discover are joining forces, while Mastercard and Visa are leaning into new tech and crypto. But Amex stands apart because it focuses on customers who spend a lot and tend to pay their bills on time. Amex makes money from both fees and interest, which helps steady its performance when rates go up or when people start feeling more pressure on their finances. The push to connect with Millennials, Gen-Z and international cardholders is paying off, too, helping Amex stay ahead of rivals.

It’s true that tech-driven companies could make things harder for Amex in the future. So far, though, Amex has managed to turn new tech into an advantage, using better data and partnerships to keep customers engaged. It’s picking up business overseas and among younger people, giving it a pretty long runway for growth.

The way I see it, Amex’s reputation and global reach are powerful. When people want to spend money safely and earn rewards, they trust brands like this one. I wouldn’t count on disruption hitting Amex the way it will hit less-established names.

The Financials Back Up the Story

In the third quarter, Amex grew its revenue by 11 percent, bringing in $18.4 billion. Earnings per share jumped 19 percent to $4.14 and return on equity hit 36 percent. The company now expects revenue to grow between 9 and 10 percent this year, with earnings per share in the range of $15.20 to $15.50, a target it just bumped up after the latest results.

Spending by U.S. consumers is up 9 percent and the numbers are even better for Millennials and Gen-Z, with growth as high as 39 percent year over year. International spending is also on the rise and more people are choosing cards that come with a fee, which helps boost average revenue per card. Business volume across Amex’s network keeps trending higher.

The company’s loan losses and delinquencies have barely budged, staying low at about 1.3 percent for delinquencies and 1.9 percent in net write-offs (2.2 percent including interest and fees). That’s thanks to Amex’s high-quality customer base. The balance sheet looks strong, with about $55 billion in cash on hand, a good cushion of deposits and a manageable level of debt. Amex keeps returning money to shareholders, both through dividends and buybacks, sending back $2.9 billion last quarter.

What stands out to me is how Amex keeps investing in things that make a difference for customers, like better rewards, tech upgrades and more ways to use their cards. Even as costs are going up, they are investing in areas that should pay off down the road.

Why the Stock Isn’t Cheap but Still Worth Buying

There’s no denying that Amex trades at a higher price than a lot of its peers. The stock is priced at about 23 times this year's expected earnings. Capital One, for comparison, trades much lower. But when you look at Amex’s returns and its steady profits, that premium starts to make sense. Its price-to-book ratio is up there, too but it matches the company’s high return profile.

Wall Street expects earnings to keep rising, with 2025 forecasts at $15.33 per share and further increases in the next couple of years. The current price makes sense if Amex keeps hitting its goals and maybe even beats them.

This isn’t a stock to buy if you want something cheap. It’s a buy for those who care about quality and growth over time. If the market takes a breather or we see a pullback, I think that’s a chance to add to your position, not a reason to sell.

What’s Driving Investor Sentiment Right Now

Amex recently had a blowout quarter, beating expectations on both revenue and profits. The mood among investors is positive but not over the top. Analysts have pointed out that the stock isn’t a bargain but most agree that management keeps delivering. After some minor dips related to worries about rates and consumer credit, the stock bounced back strongly once the company posted strong Q3 results.

Investors right now seem generally upbeat about Amex. They like that the company keeps raising its targets and growing customer spending, all while keeping losses low. Still, there’s some caution in the air, especially after recent broker downgrades that point to the higher price-to-earnings ratio. The market still expects Amex to keep executing but there’s an awareness that the bar is high.

In my opinion, as long as Amex keeps showing it can grow cards and manage its costs, sentiment will stay positive. If there’s a stumble, that’s probably an opportunity for patient investors, not a reason to run away.

Risks to Watch and Why They’re Manageable

The biggest one is probably the price. If Amex misses expectations or the economy takes a sudden downturn, the stock could take a hit because it’s priced for strong results. Other threats include shifts in consumer spending, new tech competitors who might pull away younger users and potential rule changes around fees or privacy.

That said, Amex has built in some natural defenses. Its customer base tends to be more stable, it invests in new technology and it relies less on risky loans than a lot of other financial companies. If something unexpected does happen, like a spike in losses or a surprise from regulators, that’s worth watching. For now, though, I see these as manageable bumps rather than show-stoppers.

What Could Move the Needle from Here

Looking out over the next few quarters and beyond, there are plenty of reasons to be optimistic. I expect Amex to keep growing revenue in that 9 to 10 percent range, keep bringing in new, younger cardholders and keep rolling out product and tech updates. As the company grows around the world and expands into new partnerships, there’s a lot of room to keep building the business.

Over the longer term, international markets and new digital relationships (like with Toast) could add meaningful growth. The company is getting better at using its data, which helps it find new ways to make money and serve customers. If Amex can keep up this pace, the street’s expectations for rising profits and revenue over the next few years are realistic, maybe even a little conservative.

The bar is high but Amex keeps proving it can jump over it. For long-term investors, the main thing is to keep an eye on new card growth, profit margins and how well the company keeps up with the digital changes in the payments world.

How I’m Framing the Possible Outcomes

If things go wrong, like a big slowdown in new cards or a wave of competition from fintech companies, Amex’s earnings could stall. That would probably push the stock down, as investors rethink what the company is worth. If Amex just does what’s expected, delivering steady card growth and stable profits, then the stock should stick around its current range, maybe drifting higher in line with earnings growth. The real upside comes if Amex beats expectations by signing up more customers, especially overseas and among younger people, while keeping costs in check. In that case, I could see the stock pushing above $400 within a year or two.

From where I sit, the odds favor a steady climb, with the chance for bigger gains if management really nails it. There’s more upside than downside if you’re in it for the long haul.

Why I Think Amex Still Belongs in a Long-Term Portfolio

To wrap it up, I still think American Express is a buy for people looking to hold a quality stock for years, not months. It’s not a bargain but it’s a proven winner that keeps finding ways to grow and reward shareholders.

There’s a lot of talk about new competitors or economic worries but Amex’s reputation and loyal customers make it tough to bet against. The company’s focus on the premium end of the market is still paying off and its ability to roll out new perks and tech shows it’s not standing still.

If you’re thinking long term, I see Amex as a solid part of any portfolio. It’s worth keeping an eye on the usual things, like new customer growth and profit margins but if Amex keeps doing what it’s been doing, I think the stock will keep working for you. For me, it’s about quality, trust and sticking with a company that knows how to win over time.

In short, I would buy American Express and look to add more if the market gives you a chance. This is the kind of business you want to own when you care about steady growth and a brand that stands the test of time.

data.title: AT&T: Why I'm Still Buying The High-Yielding Preferred Stock
data.description:
Introduction

The share price of AT&T (NYSE:T) has been under pressure lately as the telecom sector in the United States is changing. In this excellent preview by fellow author Vladimir Dimitrov, the key elements to look out for in next week’s financial reports are clearly outlined. That being said, I remain a big fan of the fixed-income securities that have been issued by AT&T, as there are two issues of preferred stock outstanding, as well as a long-term debt security. And although I'm obviously also still waiting to see the actual results of the third quarter (out next week), I feel very confident in picking up both types of securities, which offer yields in excess of 6%, now.

A very robust result means the preferred dividends are well covered

As I'm mainly looking at AT&T from the perspective of a preferred shareholder, I'm of course very interested in seeing how well the preferred dividends are covered by the net profits and the cash flows.

While the company still has to report on its Q3, I'm not expecting a whole lot of surprises compared to the first two quarters of the current financial year. And looking back at the results of the second quarter of this year, we see the company reported total revenue of approximately $30.8B, which represents an increase of just over 3% compared to the second quarter of last year. Meanwhile, the operating expenses increased by less than 2%, which indeed means that the operating income jumped by almost 15%. Keep in mind the second quarter of 2024 also contained some non-recurring items like the $480 million impairment and restructuring charge. This indeed also means that on an underlying basis the operating expenses increased in 2025, but the financial performance remains very robust.

As the income statement above also shows, AT&T reported a pre-tax income of approximately $6.1B, which resulted in a net profit of $4.86B, of which $4.5B was attributable to AT&T. The income statement also shows there was a 36 million preferred stock dividend and redemption gain, which resulted in an underlying net profit of just under $4.5B for an EPS of $0.62. And looking at the performance in the entire first semester, the EPS was $1.22.

As you can notice in the image, the company combines the actual cost of the preferred dividends with the redemption gain related to buying back preferred stock on the open market at the discount to the principal value. During the first half of the year, the company repurchased just over $2 billion in preferred equity. In any case, the income statement shown above clearly shows the preferred dividends enjoy absolutely excellent coverage, as the company generally needs a low-single-digit percentage of its net profit to cover the preferred share dividends.

The risk/reward ratio of the preferred stock remains appealing

AT&T currently has two series of preferred shares outstanding, and the current yield of both series is approximately the same right now. I'm focusing on the Series C preferred stock (T.PR.C) which has the lowest preferred dividend coupon of 4.7%. As the current yield is the same as the Series A preferred shares, I tend to focus on the preferred stock that's trading at a higher discount to the principal value, as in this case it means the call risk is lower than higher yielding securities.

As you can see below, the series C cumulative preferred shares issued by AT&T are currently trading at $19.60. This means the current preferred different yield is approximately 6%.

Considering the risk free interest rate is approximately 3.59% for the US 5Y Treasury and 4.00% for the US 10-year Treasury, I think the markup of 220 bps based on the midpoint of the 5-year and 10-year Treasury rates is quite attractive.

Investment thesis

I have started to build a long position in both series of the preferred shares issued by AT&T, as well as the baby bonds that are also publicly tradeable. I like the risk/reward ratio offered by these preferred and debt securities despite the recently announced multi-billion-dollar deal to acquire spectrum licenses from Echostar for $23B. While that will have an initial negative impact on the balance sheet, it will allow AT&T to pursue additional opportunities.

Meanwhile, I continue to write (out of the money) put options on the common shares, as I would still be interested in initiating a long position.

data.title: Plains All American: The Market Is Sleeping On This High Yield Gem
data.description:
Plains All American Pipeline (NASDAQ:PAA) is an $11 billion market cap midstream partnership, which is a direct peer to such MLP gems as Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD), MPLX LP (MPLX), and Western Midstream (WES).

PAA has also been one of my favorite MLP picks since April, 2024, when I published a rather bullish case on PAA's distribution offering, which, at that time, was certainly, in my view, the best in the entire MLP (AMLP) universe. PAA offered a FWD yield of 9%, which was a) backed by 190% cash flow coverage and b) supported by the management's commitment to increase it until the coverage metric normalizes at 160%. So, it was a de-risked high-yield play, which has so far worked out nicely. The quarterly distributions have increased from $0.32 per unit to $0.38 per unit, and, as I will elaborate below, we have a strong basis to count on additional hikes.

However, when speaking of income and its growth, it is always very important to pay attention to the underlying fundamentals and cash flow generation power to understand whether all of this will really stick or whether it is just a short-term party.

If we look at the historical distribution dynamics, then the chart below is not that encouraging, to say the least:

PAA has made several cuts before. Meanwhile, peers like EPD and MPLX have managed to preserve their distribution levels across many different cycles.

I think that this is also one of the key drivers for PAA's discount (EV/EBITDA of 8.7x - lower than for most other competitors) and thus above-average yield of about 9.5%.

Yet, let me now elaborate on some recent news and share my thoughts on why I believe that this time things are different with PAA. I know that by saying the phrase "this time it's different" it already sounds suspicious/risky, but bear with me.

Thesis Review

Let's first look at the key figures from Q2, 2025.

Remember, PAA's business is comprised of two parts: crude oil and NGL. The former explains the lion's share of total EBITDA generation, while the latter is more or less a complementary segment.

The core—crude oil—showed yet again strong performance by delivering quarter-to-quarter growth. The quarterly EBITDA ticked up to $580 million from $559 million in Q1. The increase was driven by Permian volume growth and the previously realized bolt-on transactions.

The NGL segment continued to struggle. There is a significant seasonality in play, which accounted for the ~$96 million drop. But even excluding the seasonality component, the adjusted EBITDA decreased by $6 million from the previous quarter, landing now at $87 million.

However, due to the exceptional performance within the core business, PAA has nicely kept the full-year 2025 EBITDA guidance in place - at around $2.9 billion, which is clearly supportive to both current and future (increased) distributions.

Why is this my assumption? Because of the following:

  • The distribution coverage remained very strong at ~175%.
  • Leverage ratio remained in the investment grade territory of 3.3x.
  • Going forward there will be tailwinds for the cash flow growth due to a quite busy period of M&A activity (see explanation below).

In Q2, PAA ventured into a bolt-on acquisition of an additional 20% interest in BridgeTex Pipeline Company LLC - paying $100 million. Since this is already a cash flow producing joint venture with ONEOK (OKE), we should expect a positive impact already in Q3. With this, on a YTD basis, PAA will have already acquired 5 bolt-on M&A moves with a total consideration of around $800 million. And even with this context, PAA's leverage is still robust at 3.3x.

Apart from this, we obviously have to mention PAA's decision to simplify the business by divesting the NGL arm. From this transaction, PAA is set to access about $3 billion of pure cash after covering deal related taxes and expenses, and, importantly, after paying out a special distribution of $0.35 per unit.

While the deal itself was conducted at an EV/EBITDA of 8.5x - value neutral given PAA's multiple - there are numerous qualitative benefits that PAA will get out of this.

First, part of the proceeds will be directed towards retiring preferreds, which will be positive for distribution coverage. Also, given that Series A preferreds yield a fixed rate of 9.37% and Series B yield SOFR + 4.37%, there should not be any material drag on the current DCF generation.

Second, PAA's cash flow conversion rate is set to improve from 70% to 80% (due to reduced CapEx needs and optimized taxation).

Third, the commodity exposure in PAA's EBITDA will go down from 20% to 15%.

Finally, PAA will now have access to a sizable war chest that could be put to work in specific segments that go hand in hand with its current core business and the overall strategy of keeping the commodity exposure as low as possible.

Risks

Here I would like to remind you what I laid out in the article from the very start. PAA cut its distribution several times ago, which is not a good sign for the sustainability of the current income streams. Even though the previous cuts were largely driven by excessive debt and unfortunate timing from the commodity market perspective (debt part is largely solved due to the IG balance sheet), we still have to appreciate PAA’s exposure to crude oil. I think that you will agree with me that the crude oil segment has become quite vulnerable now due to recessionary risks and, at the same time, strong production volumes. The prices are nowhere near where they were 2 to 3 years ago, which motivated upstream players to venture into sizable new drilling (CAPEX) projects. Now, the prices have come down, but the consequences (fresh supply) have obviously remained. So, if we suddenly experience a real economic slowdown or, say, a peace deal between Ukraine and Russia, the chances for oil dropping significantly lower would increase dramatically (and thus pressure on crude oil players). Yet, the good news is that if we look deeper into PAA’s cash generation profile, we will notice that there are extremely favorable price and volumetric production mechanisms embedded in the Partnership. About 80%+ of EBITDA is linked to take-or-pay contracts, which come with minimum base cash flows that not only are sufficient to cover debt service, but also generate accretive IRR even if commodity markets turn really south.

In essence, I see PAA’s profile de-risked on the financial and business risk front. Granted, in case of a steep correction in the crude oil segment, PAA will suffer, but not to an extent where we would be talking about distribution cut.

The Bottom Line

PAA's key fundamentals indicate that the business is in great shape and durable enough to withstand potential shocks. We are talking here about an investment-grade balance sheet, huge surplus in the distribution coverage, and improved business (on many fronts) after NGL divestiture. In previous instances when PAA was forced to cut distributions, the balance sheet was nowhere near the current level, and the exposure to commodity risk was significantly higher.

Now, what I really like about PAA is that it offers one of the sector-leading yield levels of around 9.5%, which on a forward basis (after factoring in the forthcoming special distribution) is 11.8%. So, by buying PAA, we can lock in a forward double-digit yield, where it is very likely that the actual yield will be even higher due to the potential distribution hike decisions. This is my base assumption considering management's commitment to getting the distribution coverage to 160%, which currently is around 175%.

And, of course, all of this is underpinned by resilient fundamentals.

In my humble opinion, PAA is a clear buy for income-focused investors.

data.title: Legacy Education: The Opportunity To Buy It Cheaply
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Note: This is an update to my previous article where I covered the LGCY business model in more detail.

Legacy Education (NYSE:LGCY) has medical schools and benefits from the shortage of healthcare professionals in the United States and has benefited from the fragmentation of this sector through M&A in addition to growing organically by adding new study programs.

In my previous coverage, the company reported strong results for the third quarter of 2025, triggering a nearly 50% rally, only to fall 30% in recent days due to lower-than-expected profitability in the most recent quarter. That said, there are strong indications that this could be temporary, and I believe the stock has great value at its current price, so it's worth clarifying why the market's negative reaction occurred and why it's a clear opportunity to buy at a low price.

Disappointing Profitability

On September 25, Legacy reported its fourth-quarter results, closing out fiscal year 2025. This quarter saw solid sales growth and a degree of operational stability, but with slight pressure on earnings per share, which the market didn't like at all, the shares fell almost 20% in a single day.

During the quarter, revenues totaled $18.0 million, beating consensus estimates by 7%. EBIT was $1.99 million, in line with expectations, but earnings per share were $0.09, below the $0.11 expected. With these results, the company achieved revenues of $64 million for the year, a 39.5% increase year over year, with net income of $7.5 million, representing a net margin of around 12%. Despite disappointing profitability during the fourth quarter, diluted EPS was $0.59, slightly higher than the $0.53 of the previous year.

Q4 2025 Estimate Actual Beat/miss
Revenue $16.5M $18.0M +6.9%
EBIT $1.98M $1.99M +0.5%
EPS $0.11 $0.09 -18.2%

Regarding profitability, it's worth noting that Legacy closed the year close to the highest gross margin since 2019, but operating expenses were double the previous year ($6.3 million vs. $3.3 million). Management comments that an increase of $700,000 was mainly due to a one-time increase in reserves for student accounts receivable. This happened because management noticed signs that graduates were paying more slowly than usual and decided to create a provision for bad debts. However, this doesn't mean the company lost that money, and if students pay later, that amount could be reversed.

If we add this amount to net income to slightly normalize the valuation, the company would have earned $1.93 million in the fourth quarter instead of $1.23 million. This equates to $8.24 million in net income for the full year 2025, which, when divided by the current market capitalization of $116 million, would result in a P/E ratio of 20 times 2025 earnings. Not bad for a company that grew sales 40% during that year.

Growth Prospects Are Still Positive

Beyond these accounting adjustments, Legacy closed the year with positive growth prospects. The company reached a record enrollment of 3,101 students, a 42% increase over the previous year, driven by demand for health-related programs. This performance confirms the successful integration of Contra Costa College, which added 468 students and 14 new programs, and it's positive considering possible future acquisitions and the integration that these would entail.

The company also received initial accreditation from the National League for Nursing Commission on Nursing Education Accreditation (NLN CNEA) for its Registered Nursing program, an achievement that expands its nursing education portfolio and demonstrates the company's credibility and reputation with regulatory bodies. At the same time, the company obtained approval for three new degree programs and two certificate programs, scheduled to launch in October 2025, which will be important for attracting new students through increased coursework options.

Valuation

Now that we know there's nothing wrong with the company and the overall results look good, I think it's reasonable to estimate that the company will grow at least 20% over the next few years and maintain net margins of 12% (during Q1 and Q3 2025 these reached 15%). This means that in five years, it would be earning close to $20 million in profit, divided by the current 12.7 million shares outstanding, results in EPS of $1.50. Apply a P/E of 15 times, and the shares could be worth more than double the current price. Now imagine what it could be worth if the company grows at recent rates or improves its net margins.

Risks

The most immediate risk is the company's cost structure. General and administrative expenses often increase due to marketing expenditures to attract new students in a highly competitive market with relatively little differentiation and professional fees to retain quality teachers. While some of these costs are nonrecurring and may be subject to cost controls, sustained expense growth could put pressure on margins if revenue growth normalizes.

Legacy still operates primarily in six campuses across California, exposing it to regional economic problems and potential regulatory changes at the state level. Geographic concentration remains a key strategic limitation until the company executes new acquisitions outside the state and proves that it has the necessary know-how.

Also, I'd like to point out that share dilution has been significant, with the diluted share count up 31% year over year during the full year 2025. If the company continues to use stock for acquisitions or executive compensation, EPS growth may lag behind operating income, reducing shareholder returns even if there's solid revenue growth.

The Bottom Line

Although profitability was reduced during Q4 2025, and the reason may suggest that students are struggling to pay their tuition, I think it's too early to render a verdict, and the rest of the operating performance still looks healthy and solid. If we take the current valuation into account, it appears that the recent price drop provided another buying opportunity after the stock doubled between April and September and had stopped looking so cheap.
